3.10. We recommended and Management agreed to require:
a. The Inventory committee to conduct a complete physical count of
properties, prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant,
and Equipment, and submit a copy to the Audit Team not later than
January 31 of each year;
b. The Property Office to prepare property cards for the properties included
in the accounting records but were not recorded in the property records;
c. The Accounting and Property Office to reconcile the physical inventory of
Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) with their respective records, verify
and analyze the discrepancies, and make the necessary adjustments; and
d. The Municipal Accountant to prepare journal entries to record property
items amounting to ₱640,700.00 in the appropriate PPE account and
compute the corresponding depreciation.

3.11. We further recommended and Management agreed to require:
a. The Property Office to continue exerting efforts to locate the necessary
documents to establish ownership of the four parcels of land recorded in
the books of accounts. If no records can be located, coordinate with other
government offices such as the Municipal Assessor’s Office, the Bureau
of Lands and the Department of Environment and National Resources, for
possible sources of the documentation;
b. The Appraisal Committee to determine the fair value of the donated motor
vehicles, and upon receipt of the appraised values, the Municipal
Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to recognize the
asset in the books of accounts in accordance with IPSAS 17 and 23; and
c. The Property Office to follow up on the requested revision of the
conditions stated in the Deed of Donation, and to emphasize that removal
of external markings is necessary to ensure compliance with the
prohibitions imposed by DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2010-101.

5.7. We recommended and Management agreed to direct the:
a. Municipal Accounting Office to observe the perpetual inventory method
in recording the receipt and issuance of supplies and materials;
b. Supply Officer to ensure that all issuances of supplies and materials are
documented with a Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS); and
c. Supply Officer to conduct regular assessments of supply usage per office
and implement a centralized tracking system to ensure that only the
required quantities are distributed based on actual needs.

5.8. We further recommended and Management agreed that the Property Office
and the Accounting Office:
a. Prepare Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards for each item of
inventory and ensure these records are consistently and regularly
updated to reflect acquisitions and issuances;
b. Reconcile the physical inventory of supplies and the stock cards with the
balances reported in the books of accounts and effect adjustments or
corrections, if warranted; and
c. Ensure that RISs are consolidated weekly using the Summary of Supplies
and Materials Issued to serve as basis in recording inventory issuances
in the appropriate expense accounts.

6.7. We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. Identify the cause(s) of the discrepancies and effect the necessary
adjustments in the respective books of accounts; and
b. Regularly review intra-agency transactions and ensure the reconciliation
and elimination of intra-agency accounts in the consolidated financial
statements.

7.7. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
a. Ensure that the balances in the Reconciliation Statement of Budget and
Actual Amounts to Financial Performance in the Notes to Financial
Statement align with the Statement of Financial Performance, and provide
the necessary disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements for any
noted variances; and
b. Disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements a reconciliation of the
Surplus (Deficit) from the Statement of Financial Performance to the Net
Cash Flows from Operating Activities.

8.8. We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Reconcile and disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements any
discrepancies among the Statement of Sources and Application of Funds,
Statement of Receipts and Expenditures, and Statement of Financial
Position; and
b. Transfer to the General Fund the amounts of ₱316,426.75, ₱39,576.74, and
₱79,324.56 from the Loan Proceeds, Government of the Philippines
Equity, and Local Government Unit Equity bank accounts, respectively,
and prepare the necessary journal entries.

9.8. We recommended Management agreed to require the Municipal Accounting
and Treasury Office to:
a. Immediately transfer the cash amounting to ₱2,351,423.75, representing
the unexpended balance of the CY 2024 LDRRMF, to the Trust Fund bank
account;
b. Prepare the necessary journal entry to adjust the excess transfer of
₱2,829.44 from the CY 2024 unexpended LDRRMF to the Special Trust
Fund, to correct the affected account balances in the financial statements;
c. Update the LDRRMF subsidiary records to ensure that the balances
reconcile with the amounts disclosed in the Notes to Financial
Statements; and
d. Henceforth, ensure that the actual cash of the current year’s unexpended
LDRRMF is transferred to the Trust Fund bank account at year-end to
maintain proper fund classification and utilization, in compliance with
COA Circular No. 2012-002.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the:
a. Inventory committee to conduct complete physical count of properties, prepare the RPCPPE, and submit a copy to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year; 
b. Municipal Treasurer to prepare property cards (PC) for the properties included in the accounting records but were not taken up in the property records; and
c. Accounting Office and Treasury Office reconcile the physical inventory of PPE with the balances reported in the books of accounts and property records, verify and analyze the discrepancies, and make adjustments as appropriate.
We further recommended that Management require the: a. Municipal Assessor to provide copies of the titles and/or tax declarations for the three parcels of land, which include the present municipal building site, Adaoay school site, and Kabayan Barrio school site, for verification by the Audit Team; b. Municipal Accountant to review the account history of the recorded parcels of land classified as Adaoay school site and Kabayan Barrio school site and collaborate with the accountants of the schools to verify if the school sites were recorded in their books. If so, derecognize the recorded land from the books of accounts of the Municipality; and c. GSO to exert efforts in locating the necessary documents to establish ownership of the other four parcels of land recorded in the books of accounts. In case no records can be located, coordinate with other government offices for possible sources of the documents such as, the Municipal Assessor’s Office, the Bureau of Lands and the Department of Environment and National Resources.
observations.managementAction: The physical count was conducted and the RPCPPE prepared and submitted. The Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Treasurer furnished tax declarations for the three parcels of land and the Accounting Office confirmed with the schools that the sites were not recorded in their records. The Municipal Treasurer and Assessor are working together to locate necessary documents to prove ownership of the land recorded in the books of accounts.
observations.reason: Some documents were not provided earlier; reconciliation and verification have since been undertaken and records updated where applicable.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count of inventories semestrally and prepare the corresponding RPCI, copy furnished to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year; 
b. Consider designating a Supply and/or Property officer under the supervision of the Municipal Treasurer to handle functions supporting supply and property management; and
c. Require concerned personnel to ensure that all issuances of supplies and materials are documented with a Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS).
We further recommended that Management require the Property Office and the Accounting Office:
a. Prepare Stock Cards (SCs) and Supplies Ledger Cards (SLCs) for each item of inventory and ensure that said records are consistently and regularly updated whenever there are acquisitions and issuances; 
b. Reconcile the physical inventory of supplies and the stock cards with the balances reported in the books of accounts and effect adjustment or correction, if warranted; and 
c. Ensure that RISs are consolidated using the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) weekly to serve as basis in recording inventory issuances in the appropriate expense accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee conducted physical counts semestrally and prepared the RPCI; a Supply Officer was appointed and assumed functions; SCs and SLCs were prepared though some were incomplete and not regularly updated; the SSMI and RIS consolidation procedures are being strengthened.
observations.reason: Initial deficiencies were addressed; however some record-keeping processes continue to be improved for consistent implementation.

observations.managementAction: Only cash advances for official travel were granted to elected officials; a designated disbursing officer performed the disbursing functions; cash advances were liquidated on time; new cash advances are granted only to those whose previous advances were liquidated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure that cash advances granted to disbursing officers do not exceed their respective maximum cash accountabilities as prescribed by TC No. 2019-002. Else, increase the fidelity bond of the accountable officer, to an amount sufficient to cover his maximum cash accountability.
observations.managementAction: Management increased the fidelity bonds of disbursing officers and ensured that all cash advances granted remain within their prescribed maximum cash accountability.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Prepare an adjusting journal entry to reflect in the books of accounts the shares of the DPWH and OBO from the collection of building permit fees from CY 2013-2023, to correct the balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements; and
b. Henceforth, recognize in the books of accounts the shares of the OBO and DPWH from collection of building permit fees through the suggested journal entry: Cash-in Local Treasury xxx; Trust Liabilities xxx (15% OBO); Due to NGAs xxx (5% DPWH); Permit Fees xxx (80% GF).
We further recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Reflect the breakdown of the shares of the LGU, OBO and the DPWH from collections of the building permit fees and other fees in the Official Receipt; and
b. Remit to the Bureau of Treasury within the prescribed period the five per cent share of the DPWH, and regularly monitor collections of income from building permits to ensure timely remittance of the DPWH share.
observations.managementAction: The shares of the DPWH and OBO from building permit collections from CY 2013-2023 were recorded in April 2024 under JEV No. GF2024-04-720. Remittance of the 5% DPWH share was recorded under JEV No. TF2024-04-083 and TF2024-04-082 dated April 29, 2024. The breakdown of shares is now specified in the Official Receipts and monthly remittances are being made to the Bureau of Treasury.

Several unserviceable properties were surrendered to the Property Officer; IIRUP and IIRUSP were prepared and furnished to the Disposal Committee; disposal of unserviceable properties is being undertaken following prescribed procedures.
observations.reason: Some unserviceable properties remained undisposed pending completion of required documentation and disposal procedures, but actions to update reports and effect disposals have been undertaken.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the concerned contractors to:
a. Immediately execute corrective measures to address the identified defects in the completed PCCP and to undertake restoration of the damaged riprap, with aggregate cost of ₱416,213.31; and
b. Complete the project "Improvement along Nandupit-Babalak Farm to Market Road" according to the original plan; otherwise, deduct from the retention money the excess payment based on the actual work completed, which is at 87.78 per cent, taking into account the deficiencies identified during inspection.
We further recommended that Management require the Municipal Engineer to submit all relevant documents concerning the actions taken to rectify/restore the works for further evaluation of the Audit Team and for re-inspection by the COA Technical Audit Group.
observations.managementAction: Management issued letters to the contractors directing the immediate implementation of corrective measures. The contractor deducted the excess payment from the project retention money and recorded under JEV Nos. GF2025-09-001814 dated September 30, 2025. Infrastructure inspectorate team continues to coordinate with them for compliance. Project completion monitoring is ongoing.
observations.reason: Corrective measures were instructed and excess payments deducted from retention; however documentation of corrective measures and re-inspection reports are being gathered.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Engineers to ensure that:
a. The ABC prepared for subsequent projects are in accordance with DPWH DO No. 197, series of 2016; and
b. The unit costs of materials used in their estimates align with the Construction Materials Price Data (CMPD) to enhance cost-effectiveness in materials procurement.
observations.managementAction: The MEO prepared the ABC for upcoming projects in compliance with DPWH DO No. 197 and ensured material costs matched the CMPD.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management recoup the excessive contract cost of ₱23,769.99 from the contractor by deducting the amount from any outstanding claim of the contractor. We further recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Engineer strictly comply with applicable estimating guidelines in the preparation of ABCs to avoid excessive contract costs.
observations.managementAction: The excessive contract cost of ₱23,769.99 was deducted from the claim of the contractor and recorded under JEV Nos. GF2024-10-2169 and MDF2024-10-2170 dated October 8, 2024. The MEO ensures applicable estimating guidelines are followed in the preparation of ABC.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Development Council (MDC) to:
a. Conduct proper validation of identified or proposed projects, including site inspection, to ensure that only feasible PPAs are included in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP); and
b. Carefully evaluate feasibility of the projects, especially those that have not been started. If not viable, realign the corresponding budgetary allocations to other priority projects in the AIP.
We further recommended that Management:
a. Advise the BAC to fast track bidding and procurement of unimplemented projects; and
b. Require the Municipal Engineer and the MDC to strictly monitor progress of works and facilitate timely implementation of infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction: The MDC and MEO conducted validations and re-appropriations where applicable. Budgets for PPAs deemed not feasible were re-aligned to other priority PPAs; some PPAs were included in the AIP. The BAC is trying to expedite procurement processes and the MEO monitors project implementation and coordinates inspectorate team actions.
observations.reason: Some PPAs were realigned and others remain unimplemented or pending due to feasibility issues, procurement delays and monitoring gaps.

observations.managementAction: LDRRMF PPAs were monitored by the MDRRMC; some budgets for non-feasible PPAs were re-aligned; liquidated damages of ₱18,351.95 were deducted from a contractor’s claim for a delayed LDRRMF project; and an LDRRMFIP was prepared and updates undertaken.
observations.reason: Some PPAs were not fully implemented; LDRRMF planning and full allocation of prior year balances into AIP required improved coordination and monitoring.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to:
a. Ensure compliance with the administration of the HGDG tests on its PPAs to determine the cost of gender mainstreaming and actual expenditures to be attributed to GAD budget and GAD expenditures, respectively; and
b. Enjoin all members of the GFPS and GAD Technical Working Group (TWG) to be capacitated on the GAD tools, specifically on the HGDG tests.
We further recommended that the GFPS submit a copy of the DILG endorsed GPB and the corresponding GAD Accomplishment Report to the Audit Team within the period prescribed under COA Circular No. 2014-001.
observations.managementAction: HGDG tests were conducted and a GAD Technical Workshop was held February 25-27, 2025. The GPB and GAD Accomplishment Report were prepared and submitted to the Audit Team as required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the GFPS to fully implement the GAD PPAs for which it is responsible as specified in the approved PCW-endorsed GAD Plan and Budget.
observations.managementAction: GAD PPAs were implemented where feasible; certain PPAs were executed and monitored, while five PPAs remained unimplemented due to scheduling, targeting and coordination issues.
observations.reason: Some GAD PPAs were not implemented due to operational issues; monitoring and coordination are being enhanced.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management revert the excess PRDP-LGU Equity funds amounting to ₱1,509,508.64, from the Trust Fund account to the General Fund account to be used for other purposes that will benefit the Municipality’s constituents.
observations.managementAction: The excess PRDP-LGU Equity funds amounting to ₱1,509,508.64 were transferred to the General Fund account and recorded under JEV No. TF2024-04-084 (Equity) dated April 29, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the BAC to ensure that henceforth, observers are invited in all stages of the procurement process pursuant to Section 13 of the IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Observers are invited by the BAC in all stages of procurement.

DPWH standards/guidelines are being followed by the MEO in the preparation of POWs for current and succeeding infrastructure projects. No new PRDP-IBuild subproject has been proposed or implemented by the MLGU since.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Transfer the amount of ₱20,000.00 from the PRDP-LP and PRDP-GOP bank accounts to the LGU-Equity bank account and prepare the necessary journal entries; and
b. Update the cash balances presented in the SSAF/SRE to reconcile with those presented in the SFPo.
observations.managementAction: The amount of ₱20,000.00 was transferred to the LGU-equity bank account and recorded under JEV Nos. TF2024-04-082 and TF2024-04-083 dated April 29, 2024. Cash balances in the SSAF and SRE were updated and reconciled with the SFPo as of December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to analyze thoroughly the existence of the negative balances amounting to ₱80,249.80 and effect the necessary adjustments.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office prepared JEV No. TF2024-12-000301 to adjust the negative balance of the Other Payables account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to intensify efforts to enhance collection of delinquent real property taxes by conducting an intensive tax campaign in every barangay; and notify taxpayers with RPT delinquencies in the manner prescribed in Section 254 of RA 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Treasury Office conducted field collections and distributed notices of tax delinquencies to concerned taxpayers in each barangay, enhancing the collection efficiency of delinquent RPT.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Assessor and Treasurer to reconcile the Real Property Assessment Roll and the RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card; 
b. Take necessary measures to identify properties with duplicate tax declarations and remove them from the records to ensure that only valid properties are included in the computation of real property taxes; and
c. Require the Municipal Treasurer to compute the RPT and SET Receivable based on the updated RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card and provide the accountant at the start of the year with a certified list showing the name of the taxpayer and the corresponding amount due and collectible for the year.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to analyze the difference between the RPT/SET Receivables recorded in the books of accounts and the Report of RPT delinquencies, and effect appropriate adjustments, if warranted; and set-up RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the year based on the certified list provided by the MTO.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasury are reconciling their respective records and updating the RPTAR and computation of tax due and collectible for CY 2024. The Accounting Office started setting up the RPT/SET receivables based on the certified list provided by the Treasury Office.
observations.reason: Reconciliation is ongoing; only about 60% of records have been reconciled to date. Duplicate declarations and other discrepancies remain under review.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to immediately demand letters to the concerned accountable officers requiring the production of the missing property items for which they are accountable, and subsequently prepare a report thereon. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in the event of AO’s non-compliance to the demand letter, draw the JEV to recognize in the books of accounts the loss of PPE and set up the corresponding receivables. We further recommended that the Accounting and Treasurer’s Office periodically reconcile their property records.
observations.managementAction: The Treasury Office submitted an updated list of accountable officers with unpresented property accountabilities to the Internal Control Unit for appropriate action. The Accounting Office started reconciling property records with the Treasury Office. Demand letters and related actions are being processed.
observations.reason: Demand letters and full reconciliation were pending at reporting; some accountable officers have yet to respond and some missing items remain under investigation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to require all revenue collectors to turn-over their collections daily and intact to the Liquidating Officer/Municipal Treasurer to enable timely accounting and deposit of funds.
observations.managementAction: Revenue collectors turned-over their collections daily and intact to the Liquidating Officer/Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Confer with the donor of the Toyota Land Cruiser to clarify with respect to the condition set in the Deed of Donation and inform him of the marking revision requirement under COA Circular No.75-6 and the prohibitions imposed by DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2010-101. If the donor consents to the removal of the prohibited markings, request that the Deed of Donation be revised to such effect. Else, decline the donation in view of the requirements and prohibitions provided by the COA and DILG Circular/Memorandum Circular; and
b. Require the Appraisal Committee to determine the fair value of the donated motor vehicle and take up in the accounting records should the donor consent to the alterations required to comply with the prescribed markings of government property.
We further recommended that Management remove the name of government personalities on the emergency vehicle received from the Provincial Government of Benguet and mark the same with the “FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY” sign pursuant to COA Circular No. 75-6.
observations.managementAction: Management conferred with the donor regarding the condition in the Deed of Donation; the donated motor vehicle was officially transferred to the municipality in June 2025 and prohibited markings have been removed. The Appraisal Committee is scheduled to convene to determine the fair value of the donated motor vehicles for proper recognition in the accounting records.
observations.reason: Response from the donor on deed revisions was pending; appraised values were not yet available at reporting time but transfer and marking removal later completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the contractors to post warranty security after the defects liability period for infrastructure projects in the form prescribed in the RIRR of R.A. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Letters were sent to contractors requiring them to post warranty security; some contractors have complied and the Municipality has started requiring warranty security for infrastructure completed following the defects liability period.
observations.reason: Warranty security was not posted for the majority of infrastructure projects executed prior to the new requirement; subsequent projects now require warranty security.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management file a request to write-off the dormant receivables, supported with pertinent documents, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: A request for write-off of the dormant receivable accounts was submitted to the Audit Team on December 26, 2024, with necessary documentation.
observations.reason: The request for write-off was submitted but supporting documentation was being compiled to fully satisfy COA requirements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-001, and henceforth submit all contracts, POs and supporting documents within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretariat submitted available executed contracts and related supporting documents to the Audit Team; some contracts and documents were incomplete or submitted late but efforts to complete the submissions continue.
observations.reason: Submission of contracts and supporting documents was incomplete and some were not submitted within the prescribed period; BAC Secretariat continues to gather and submit required documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management enforce the immediate settlement of the disallowances and charges in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006.
observations.managementAction: Continuous efforts are being exerted to collect the amount disallowed from the persons liable. For those still in government service, collections are being enforced through payroll deductions. Some collections have been made; however, collection has been difficult due to separation from service and unknown whereabouts of some accountable individuals. The person liable for the Notice of Charge has a remaining claim from the municipality which cannot be applied due to legal restrictions and lack of waiver from heirs.
observations.reason: Collections constrained by separation from service, deceased or absent persons liable, legal restrictions on applying claims against liabilities, and unavailability of some accountable individuals.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the: a. Municipal Engineer to provide the Accountant and the GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects; b. Inventory Committee to conduct an annual physical inventory of road networks and prepare the corresponding RPCLRN following the format prescribed in Annex C of COA Circular No. 2015-008; c. General Services Officer (GSO) to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components; and d. Municipal Accountant to keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network, and to record in the books of accounts the completed road network projects and determine the corresponding depreciation of each road component. We further recommended that Management ensure adherence with the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Road Asset Management System prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-008 in order to come up with a reliable Local Road Network account balance in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The Municipal Engineer did not provide the Accountant and GSO with a report detailing the description and cost segregation of road components for road projects. Not Implemented The annual physical inventory of road networks was not conducted, and no RPCLRN was prepared and submitted. Not Implemented The Municipal Treasury Office (MTO) and MAccO has yet to receive the complete description of road and cost segregation from the Municipal Engineer. Not Implemented The Municipal Accountant and MTO had yet to prepare the subsidiary records and segregation for each road component.
observations.reason: The required reports for the Local Road Network assets of the municipality were not prepared.

observations.recommendation:
1.12. We reiterated our recommendations and Management agreed to require the:
a. Municipal Engineer to provide the Accountant and the GSO with the
complete description and cost segregation per road component of road
projects;
b. Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical inventory of road
networks and prepare the corresponding RPCLRN in accordance with the
format prescribed in Annex C of COA Circular No. 2015-008;
c. General Services Officer (GSO) to maintain a Local Road Inventory and
Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all
roads and its components; and
d. Municipal Accountant to keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads
and road components of every road network, and determine the
corresponding depreciation thereof.
1.13. We further recommended and Management agreed to ensure adherence with
the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Road Asset
Management System prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-008 in order to
come up with a reliable Local Road Network account balance in the financial
statements.

c. Henceforth, abide by and observe the prescribed accounting and

4.11. We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. Ensure that the balances in the Reconciliation Statement of Budget and
Actual Amounts to Financial Performance in the Notes to Financial
Statement tallies with the Statement of Financial Performance, and provide
the necessary disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements for any
noted variances; and
b. Henceforth, disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements a reconciliation
of the Surplus (Deficit) to the Net Cash Flows from Operating Activities in
accordance with IPSAS 2.

5.12. We recommended and Management agreed to require the:
a. Inventory committee to conduct complete physical count of properties,
prepare the RPCPPE, and submit a copy to the Audit Team not later than
January 31 of each year;
b. Municipal Treasurer to prepare property cards for the properties included
in the accounting records but were not taken up in the property records;
and
c. Accounting Office and Treasury Office reconcile the physical inventory of
PPE with the balances reported in the books of accounts and property
records, verify and analyze the discrepancies, and make adjustments as
appropriate.
5.13. We further recommended and Management agreed to require the:
a. Municipal Assessor to provide copies of the titles and/or tax declarations
for the three parcels of land, which include the present municipal building
site, Adaoay school site, and Kabayan Barrio school site, for verification
by the Audit Team;
b. Municipal Accountant to review the account history of the recorded
parcels of land classified as Adaoay school site and Kabayan Barrio
school site and collaborate with the accountants of the schools to verify
if the school sites were recorded in their books. If so, derecognize the
recorded land from the books of accounts of the Municipality; and
c. General Services Office (GSO) to exert efforts in locating the necessary
documents to establish ownership of the other four parcels of land
recorded in the books of accounts. In case no records can be located,
coordinate with other government offices for possible sources of the
documents such as, the Municipal Assessor’s Office, the Bureau of Lands
and the Department of Environment and National Resources.
5.14. Management commented that they have consulted with the accountants of the
schools regarding the recorded school sites. However, the school accountants
explained that land is not recorded in their books if it lacks a title. Thus, the three
identified school sites covered by tax declarations were not recorded in the books
of the concerned schools. Also, no records were found regarding the other four
parcels of land. Nevertheless, management committed to make continuous efforts
to comply with the audit recommendations.

6.12. We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count of
inventories semestrally and prepare the corresponding RPCI, copy
furnished to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each
year;
b. Consider designating a Supply and/or Property officer under the
supervision of the Municipal Treasurer to handle functions supporting
supply and property management; and
c. Require concerned personnel to ensure that all issuances of supplies and
materials are documented with a Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS).
6.13. We further recommended and Management agreed that the Property Office
and the Accounting Office:
a. Prepare Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards for each item of inventory
and ensure that said records are consistently and regularly updated
whenever there are acquisitions and issuances;
b. Reconcile the physical inventory of supplies and the stock cards with the
balances reported in the books of accounts and effect adjustment or
correction, if warranted; and
c. Ensure that RISs are consolidated using the Summary of Supplies and
Materials Issued weekly to serve as basis in recording inventory issuances
in the appropriate expense accounts.
6.14. Management commented that a plantilla position for Supply Officer has been
created for CY 2024 and the item is pending publication. In the meantime, an
executive order has been issued to organize the Internal Control Unit, with sub-
sections designated to handle procurement, issuance, and physical inventory of
supplies and properties.

7.4. We recommended and Management agreed to ensure strict compliance with
the rules and regulations on the granting, utilization, and liquidation of cash
advances and:
a. Refrain from granting cash advances to elected officials other than for
purposes of official travel;
b. Ensure that only duly appointed or designated disbursing officers perform
disbursing functions;
c. Require all officials and employees concerned to comply with the
prescribed timeline on the liquidation of cash advances; and
d. Enforce policy of not granting additional cash advance to officials and/or
employees whose previous cash advance were not yet liquidated or not
properly accounted for.

8.8. We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that cash advances
granted to disbursing officers do not exceed their respective maximum cash
accountabilities as prescribed by Treasury Circular No. 2019-002. Else,
increase the fidelity bond of the accountable officer, to an amount sufficient
to cover his maximum cash accountability.

Accountant to:

shares of the DPWH and OBO from the collection of building permit fees
from CY 2013-2023, to correct the balances of the affected accounts in the
financial statements; and

suggested journal entry:

9.13. We further recommended and Management agreed to henceforth, require the
Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Reflect the breakdown of the shares of the LGU, OBO and the DPWH from
collections of the building permit fees and other related charges in the
Official Receipt; and
b. Remit to the Bureau of Treasury within the prescribed period the five per
cent share of the DPWH, and regularly monitor collections of income from
building permits to ensure timely remittance of the DPWH share.
9.14. Management committed to begin recording the allocation of building permit fees
in the appropriate accounts in CY 2024.

10.11. We recommended and Management agreed to direct the:
a. Concerned accountable officers to return/surrender the unserviceable
properties in their possession to the Property Officer for proper
inspection and cancellation of the corresponding Property
Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) and/or Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS);
b. Property Office to update the IIRUP to include all unserviceable property
and equipment, and prepare a separate IIRUSP for unserviceable semi-
expendable items;
c. Property Office to provide the Disposal Committee a copy of the duly
accomplished and signed IIRUP and IIRUSP; and
d. Disposal committee to promptly evaluate the unserviceable properties
and recommend to the Local Chief Executive the appropriate mode of
disposal.
10.12. We further recommended and Management agreed to require the Disposal
committee to dispose of the unserviceable properties in accordance with
prescribed rules and regulations on supply and property management.

11.9. We recommended and Management agreed to require the concerned
contractors to:
a. Immediately execute corrective measures to address the identified
defects in the completed PCCP and to undertake restoration of the
damaged riprap, with aggregate cost of ₱416,213.31; and
b. Complete the project "Improvement along Nandupit-Babalak Farm to
Market Road" according to the original plan; otherwise, deduct from the
retention money the excess payment based on the actual work completed,
which is at 87.78 per cent, taking into account the deficiencies identified
during inspection.
11.10. We further recommended that Management require the Municipal Engineer to
submit all relevant documents concerning the actions taken to rectify/restore
the defects/damages, if any, for further evaluation of the Audit Team and for
re-inspection by the COA Technical Audit Group.
11.11. Lastly, we recommended and Management agreed to henceforth, require the
Project Monitoring Committee and Infrastructure Inspectorate Team strictly
monitor completed projects, before issuance of the Final Certificate of
Acceptance and during the period covered by warranties as provided in
Section 62 of RA 9184, to ensure that construction works are accomplished
in accordance with the terms of the contract and to determine whether the
contractor is to be held liable for observed defects and/or damages.

12.9. We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Engineers to ensure that:
a. The Approved Budget for the Contract prepared for subsequent projects
are in accordance with DPWH DO No. 197, series of 2016; and
b. The unit costs of materials used in their estimates align with the
Construction Materials Price Data (CMPD) to enhance cost-effectiveness
in materials procurement.

13.7. We recommended and Management agreed to recoup the excessive contract
cost of ₱23,769.99 from the contractor by deducting the amount from any
outstanding claim of the contractor.
13.8. We further recommended and Management agreed to henceforth, require the
Municipal Engineer to strictly comply with applicable estimating guidelines
in the preparation of ABCs to avoid excessive contract costs.
13.9. Management commented that they will send written notification to the concerned
contractor to inform them of the excessive contract cost for the project.

14.10. We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Development Council (MDC) to:
a. Conduct proper validation of identified or proposed projects, including
site inspection, to ensure that only feasible PPAs, free from any issues, are
included in the AIP; and
b. Carefully evaluate feasibility of the projects, especially those that have
not been started. If not viable, realign the corresponding budgetary
allocations to other priority projects in the AIP.
14.11. We further recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Advise the BAC to fast track the bidding and procurement process of the
unimplemented projects to bring timely development in the community
for the welfare of the public; and
b. Require the Municipal Engineer and the MDC to strictly monitor the
progress of works and facilitate timely implementation of infrastructure
projects.
14.12. Management commented that the observations are duly noted and committed to
exert best efforts to implement the audit recommendations.

15.20. We recommended and Management agreed to direct the MDRRM Office and
MDRRM Council to:
a. Utilize the QRF during a state of calamity to enhance the Municipality's
capacity to respond effectively to disasters, mitigate their impacts, and
support affected communities in times of crisis.;
b. Re-appropriate the budget for PPAs which are no longer feasible to other
disaster related initiatives aligned with the municipality's disaster risk
reduction goals and priorities;
c. Properly plan and monitor the timely implementation of identified
programs/projects/activities under the LDRRM Fund;
d. Prepare a comprehensive LDRRMFIP which specifies the list of PPAs to be
funded by both current and previous years unexpended LDRRMF with the
corresponding budgetary requirements in conformance with COA Circular
No. 2012-002, and ensure that these PPAs are incorporated into the revised
AIP, approved by the local sanggunian and Local Chief Executive; and
e. Henceforth, ensure that utilization of the LDRRMF be strictly limited to
expenses that directly contribute to disaster prevention and mitigation,
preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery, as prescribed under
the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG
dated March 25, 2013.

16.8. We recommended and Management agreed to require the GFPS to:
a. Ensure compliance with the administration of the HGDG tests on its PPAs
to determine the cost of gender mainstreaming and actual expenditures
to be attributed to GAD budget and GAD expenditures, respectively; and
b. Enjoin all members of the GFPS and GAD Technical Working Group to be
capacitated on the GAD tools, specifically on the HGDG tests.
16.9. We further recommended and Management agreed to require the GFPS to
submit a copy of the DILG endorsed GPB and the corresponding GAD
Accomplishment Report to the Audit Team within the period prescribed under
COA Circular No. 2014-001.

17.4. We recommended and Management agreed to require the GFPS to fully
implement the GAD PPAs for which it is responsible as specified in the
approved PCW- endorsed GAD Plan and Budget.

18.10. We recommended and Management agreed to revert the excess PRDP-LGU
Equity funds amounting to ₱1,509,508.64, from the Trust Fund account to the
General Fund account to be used for other purposes that will benefit the
Municipality’s constituents.

19.8. We recommended and Management agreed to require the BAC to ensure that
henceforth, observers are invited in all stages of the procurement process
pursuant to Section 13 of the IRR of RA No. 9184.

Engineer:
a. Use the exact estimated duration of the activity for each work item to deter
over-estimates in the Approved Budget for the Contract and Contract
cost;
b. Strictly comply with the DPWH Standard Pay Item List in the preparation
of Program of Works and Approved Budget for the contract;
c. Ensure consistent use of unit prices for materials across different work
items outlined in the Program of Works; and
d. Use the factor one in deriving the quantity of materials pursuant to
Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) Department Order No.
207, Series of 2016 dated October 20, 2016.

observations.recommendation:
1.8 We recommended that management send a written request to DBP for the:

Account Debit Credit
Prior Years’ Adjustment 7,650.00
Cash in Bank-LCCA 7,650.00

4.8 We recommended that Management file a request to write-off the dormant receivables, supported with pertinent documents, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.

7.6.1 donated items from the Department of Health in the books of accounts and compute for the corresponding depreciation;
7.6.2 completed infrastructure projects funded from the Municipal Development Fund with aggregate project cost of ₱1,789,417.29; and
7.6.3 completed infrastructure projects with aggregate cost of ₱8,756,330.44 initially recorded under Due to NGAs to the appropriate PPE accounts and provide for the corresponding accumulated depreciation.

7.7 We further recommended that Management require the Municipality’s appraisal committee to determine the fair value of the donated PPE from private individuals for proper recording in the books.

8.12 We recommended that Management to require the Municipal Treasurer to:
8.12.1 prepare property cards for the properties included in the Accounting records but were not taken up in the property records, and cancel one of the property cards of the PPE item recorded twice; and
8.12.2 send immediately demand letters to the concern accountable officers requiring the production of the missing property items for which they are accountable.

8.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in the event of AOs’ non-compliance to the demand letters, to draw the JEV to recognize in the books of accounts the loss of PPE and to set up the corresponding receivables.

8.14 We further recommended and the Accounting and Treasurer’s Office agreed to periodically reconcile their property records.

9.6 We recommended that Management dispose of the unserviceable properties in accordance with the Handbook on Property and Supply Management as prescribed by COA Circular No. 92-386 to prevent further deterioration of the stocked items and to derive income from sale thereof.

9.7 We further recommended that the Property Officer accomplish the IIRUP properly to show the correct status/condition of each unserviceable property.

10.8 We recommended and Management agreed to:
10.8.1 Coordinate with the source agencies and request for authority to transfer the unexpended balances amounting to ₱23,429.58 to the General Fund of the Municipality. Otherwise, remit to the National Treasury or return the unexpended balance of completed PPAs with an aggregate amount of ₱329,419.58 to the source agencies, whichever is appropriate; and
10.8.2 Submit to the Audit Team the corresponding liquidation report and other pertinent documents for post-audit.

12.7 We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
12.7.1 immediately identify the cause of the discrepancies and effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts;
12.7.2 draw adjusting journal entries simultaneously in all funds affected with the intra-agency transactions; and
12.7.3 perform regular review of intra-agency transactions, reconcile reciprocal accounts and cause the elimination thereof in the consolidated financial statements.

13.9 We recommended that Management appropriate and utilize the unexpended BGCM Fund amounting to ₱28,560.71 for COVID-19 related PPAs pursuant to LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 2020. Otherwise, return the unexpended balance to the National Treasury, if no longer needed.

14.12 We recommended that Management require the Municipal Development Council (MDC) to:
14.12.1 conduct proper validation of identified or proposed projects, including site inspection, to ensure that only feasible PPAs, free from any issues, are included in the AIP; and
14.12.2 carefully evaluate feasibility of the projects, especially those that have not been started. If not viable, revert the funds to the unappropriated DF for re-appropriation to other priority projects.

14.13 We further recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Engineer and the MDC to strictly monitor progress of works and facilitate timely implementation of infrastructure projects.

15.5 We recommended and Management agreed to:
15.5.1 Require the Municipal Accountant to cause the transfer of the ₱86,700.00 savings from the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund for re-appropriation to other disaster risk reduction and management activities as required under COA Circular No. 2012-002; and
15.5.2 Henceforth, ensure that all unexpended balances from the LDRRMF are accurately and promptly transferred to the Special Trust Fund under the account Trust Liability-DRRM to facilitate efficient budgeting and utilization of LDRRMF.

16.16 We recommended and Management agreed to:
16.16.1 ensure timely implementation of identified programs/ projects/ activities to strengthen the Municipality’s capability to respond to disaster situations;
16.16.2 direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office to prepare a comprehensive LDRRMFIP which specifies the list of projects and activities to be charged to the current and unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in conformance with COA Circular No. 2012-002; and
16.16.3 allocate the LDRRMF solely to programs/ projects/ activities authorized under the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

17.5 We recommended and Management agreed to:

19.9 We recommended that Management assist the Proponent Groups in drafting a contract or MOA with its member-beneficiaries regarding their duties, responsibilities and obligations on the utilization of subproject structures, facilities and equipment to ensure continuity of benefits that may be derived therefrom.

20.10 We recommended and Management agreed to:

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality submit an explanation on the allocation and utilization of the BGCM for the Purchase of space/lot for temporary shelters of the homeless costing ₱1,500,000.00 which expenditure is not included in the allowable PPAs and expenses under LBC No. 125; a copy of the Survey Plan prepared by a Geodetic Engineer; a certification from the Department of Public Works and Highways that the purchased lot is not a portion of a Road Right of Way; and a certification from the Department of Environment and Natural Resources that the purchased lot is a portion of an alienable and disposable land.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management submit explanation to justify the procurement process of the Construction of the Ligtas Covid Center which involved an extraordinary short period of time. We further recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee submit a proof of invitation through the PhilGEPS on May 5, 2020 and proof of postings of the required documents to the GPPB Online portal.
observations.managementAction: Management explained that posting of invitation in the Philgeps on May 5, 2020 was unsuccessful due to the difficulty in accessing the portal coupled with the poor signal to access the internet. Thus, the said invitations were relayed to available local contractors at the time. It was management’s concern to target the shortest legal possible time to procure the project considering that there is no available building for isolation of COVID-19 positive and close contacts in the Municipality.
observations.reason: Proof of invitation and posting submitted to the audit team not available; posting in PhilGEPS was unsuccessful.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit an explanation to justify the construction of the Ligtas Covid Center instead of developing the basement of the separate building owned by the Municipality for purposes related to the Covid-19 pandemic.
observations.managementAction: Management explained that the Municipal Health center was not utilized as several intended buildings/structures owned by the LGU were inspected but none were found to conform to the standards of a COVID-Isolation/Quarantine Facility. Management considered renting but no area was available. The COVID center was not utilized as the primary Temporary Treatment and Monitoring Facility (TTMF) because at the time that it was completed, the Kamora NHS was already fully equipped with the essential facilities for COVID-19 response and transferring the equipment/shelters in the newly constructed COVID center will disrupt the operations and will only entail additional operating expenses. The MIATF recommended to maintain the Kamora NHS as the TTMF; hence, the COVID center was utilized for other COVID-19 response activities such as office of the contact tracing team and staging area for logistical support/supplies and to isolate health workers/front liners who were afflicted with COVID or are close contacts. Management also considered that construction of the COVID center is more economical and practical in the long run because it can be improved/utilized for other general health services and COVID response in its endemic phase.
observations.reason: Inspection revealed that proposed alternate buildings were not suitable; temporary arrangements exist; justification provided.

b. The designated LDRRM Officer will submit a LDRRM Plan yearly to the Audit Team for verification purposes.
We recommended Management to ensure that the annual funding requirements listed in the SWMP are sufficiently provided in the course of the budget process.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System, in coordination with the LPDO, develop and institutionalize the GAD Database, to include gender statistics and sex-disaggregated data, as inputs for evidence-based planning, programming and policy making pursuant to JMC 2013-01 and R.A. No. 9710.

We recommended that Management create its GAD M&E Team or expand the functions and membership of the Municipal Project Monitoring Committee to include the GFPS TWG Chair, HRM Officer, Budget Officer, and representatives from women’s organizations and the academe pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
We have recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries.

We have recommended that Management prepare amended MOA for the above mentioned fund releases to integrate all the provisions and terms as required and to submit the other lacking documents. Henceforth, incorporate in the MOA all pertinent provisions cited in the said COA circular and ensure that all required supporting documents are attached.
The savings from implemented projects amounting to P37,267.51 be reverted to the BTr.

3.7. We recommended and Management agreed to:
3.7.1. Prepare the necessary journal entries to recognize the PPE
items in the books of accounts and compute for the
corresponding depreciation; and
3.7.2. Ensure that all resources owned and controlled by the
Municipality are appropriately recorded in the books of
accounts.

4.7. We recommended that Management:
4.7.1. Strictly comply with the prescribed procedures in the receipt
and issuance of inventory in compliance with Volume 1 of the
NGAS Manual; and
4.7.2. Require the preparation and maintenance of SCs and SLCs as
control records compliant with the prescribed form and
containing the required information to: (a) aid in the control
and monitoring of inventory; (b) facilitate in the physical
inventory-taking; and (c) enable the reconciliation of the
different inventory records.

5.5. We recommended and Management agreed to:
5.5.1. Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances
of the affected accounts; and
5.5.2. Ensure that the appropriate accounts are used in the
preparation of journal entries to present reliable and accurate
financial statements.

6.6. We recommended for Management, thru the Municipal Accounting
Office, to:
6.6.1. Prepare the necessary journal entry to revert to the General
Fund the amount pertaining to the excess counterpart of the
Municipality for the SEDIP; and
6.6.2. Analyze the occurrence of the abnormal balance and effect the
necessary adjustment.

7.11. We strongly recommended that Management:
7.11.1. Set a reasonable deadline for the concerned AO to present to
the Municipal Treasurer the properties under their care. In
case of failure, immediately request the Municipal Treasurer
to prepare a Notice of Payment to be signed by the Municipal
Mayor, copy furnished the MAccO for the recording of
receivable from the concerned AO; and
7.11.2. In case of donated asset, immediately prepare appropriate
documents to support the transfer.

8.8. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, having certified
Box A of the DVs and recorded the payments in the books of
accounts, facilitate the complete documentation to support the claims,
and immediately submit the DVs to the Audit Team within the date
prescribed.

8.9. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that
DVs are supported with complete documentation before affixing his
signature in Box A of the DVs to ensure the timely submission of the
documents to the audit team.

9.7. We reiterated our previous audit recommendation for the Bids and
Awards Committee (BAC) and the BAC Secretariat, to submit
contracts entered into by the Municipality, with all its supporting
documents, to the auditor’s office within the required period to
preclude the issuance of a Notice of Suspension and the enforcement
of the penalty prescribed by law under Item 4.1 of COA Circular No.
2009-001.

10.5. We recommended and Management agreed to:
10.5.1. Regularly submit the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption
and Monthly Report of Official Travels to the Audit Team;
and
10.5.2. Require the supplier to attach the charge invoices to the
statement of account to facilitate validation of procured fuel.

11.4. We recommended and Management agreed to:
11.4.1. Return the unexpended balances of completed projects to the
respective source agencies; and
11.4.2. Submit to the Audit Team the corresponding liquidation
report and other pertinent documents for post-audit and
issuance of appropriate Credit Notice.

12.8. We recommended that Management consider the following:
12.8.1. Instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Office to
require the Program of Work before development projects are
included in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) to ensure timely
and efficient implementation;
12.8.2. Ensure that only feasible PPAs are included in the AIP.
Programs and projects with problems, issues, complaints, and
some other reasons should not be considered;
12.8.3. Formulate/develop specific strategies/solutions to effectively
implement development projects. Consider formulating a
timetable within which to start and complete the
unimplemented projects; and
12.8.4. Hire additional personnel to fill-up vacation positions in the
Municipal Engineering Office.

13.8. In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipality limit
the utilization of the SEF to the allowed expenses as prescribed by
rules and regulations.

14.9. We recommended that Management:
14.9.1. Create mandatory positions or designate employees to the
LDRRMO for administration and training, research and
planning, and operations and warning and to implement and
monitor LDRRM projects and activities; and
14.9.2. Hire a full time personnel to fill up the vacant position of the
LDRRMO so as to fully monitor LDRRMF projects and
activities.

15.5. We recommended for Management to make proper representation
with the NSWMC to facilitate the review and approval of the
Municipality’s ESWMP.

16.9. We recommended and Management agreed to the following courses of
action:
16.9.1. Strictly comply with the guidelines on the disposition and
allocation of the PFP Fund as prescribed in Item C of Annex J
of Philhealth Circular No. 002-2015 to ensure that the
provision of quality health services is maintained and further
enhanced; and
16.9.2. Prepare and submit to the Office of the Auditor the required
PFP Disposition and Allocation Form on a quarterly basis.

17.7. We recommended the following courses of action:
17.7.1. For the MHO to ensure the timely preparation of BS as
provided in Philheatlh Circular No. 2016-0013 to assure
receipt of additional funds for the provision of quality health
services;
17.7.2. For the MAccO to recognize in the books of accounts all
billed PFP claims as provided in the above guidelines as well
as to consistently apply the accrual basis of accounting on all
transactions.

18.7. We recommended that Management:
18.7.1. Require the concerned office/department to immediately
submit a written explanation to the Audit Team justifying
therein the need to procure high end mobile phones as a
modem for internet connectivity to preclude the issuance of
notice of disallowance;
18.7.2. Ensure that procurement requirements included in the APP
are only those that are considered crucial in the efficient
discharge of governmental functions; and
18.7.3. Ensure that disbursements are in accordance with the
provisions of COA Circular 2012-003.

19.3. We recommended and management agreed to:
19.3.1. Prepare the APP in conformity with the prescribed format
containing therein information that reflects the entire
procurement activity to ensure effective and efficient
procurement performance;
19.3.2. Employ competitive bidding as the primary mode of
procurement; and
19.3.3. Procurement of supplies, materials and equipment thru
shopping should be done only in cases provided in Section 52 of
RA No. 9184.

20.6. We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that the cost
of implementing GAD related activities included in the GPB be
allocated exclusively from the approved annual appropriation of the
Municipality.

21.5. We recommended and Management agreed to fully integrate and
mainstream GAD in its policy making, planning, programming and
budgeting process and to clearly indicate the cost attributed to the
GAD Budget for the implementation of the GAD related PPAs to
provide reasonable assurance that gender issues and concerns
particularly on gender equality and women’s empowerment are
properly addressed.

22.8. We recommended that the Municipality;
22.8.1. Administer the HGDG test on its PPAs, the results of which
shall be the basis in estimating the cost of gender
mainstreaming and the Gender and Development Budget;
22.8.2. Through its GAD Focal Point System, administer the
HGDG test on its GAD PPAs during the preparation of the
GAD AR as basis in determining the actual expenditure that
can be attributed to the GAD Budget pursuant to JMC No.
2013-01; and
22.8.3. Require the members of the GAD Focal Point System
(GFPS) to attend trainings on HGDG to enable them to
administer the HGDG test on the identified GAD PPAs of
the Municipality.

23.5. We recommended and management agreed to establish the LCPC
and allocate one percent of its IRA for the implementation of the
LCPC programs in compliance with RA No. 9344 or the Juvenile
Justice and Welfare Act of 2006.

24.8. We recommended that the Municipality:
24.8.1. Immediately remit to the BIR the remaining taxes withheld
totaling ₱24,127.08 in compliance with BIR RR No. 2-98
and bear/pay the corresponding surcharges and penalties
including compromise fee, if any, out of her personal funds
for her failure to remit the taxes withheld within the
prescribed period; and
24.8.2. Henceforth, comply strictly with BIR RR No. 2-98 and
remit to the BIR all taxes withheld within the ten days after
the end of each month, except for taxes withheld for
December which shall be remitted not later than January
25 of the following year, to avoid surcharges/penalties and
to enable the timely use of tax revenues to finance the
government’s various undertakings for the benefit of the
public.

25.3. We recommended that Management enforce the immediate
settlement of audit disallowances and charges.

We recommended that the Treasury Office and the Accounting Department:

c. In case of donated asset, obtain necessary documents to support such donation and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the affected PPE accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the Municipal Treasurer to immediately prepare and provide the Municipal Accounting Office with a copy of the duly accomplished and signed IIRUP to serve as basis in derecognizing unserviceable PPE items from their respective books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: The IIRUP was submitted to the disposal committee for their appropriate action. However, the MAccO was not furnished with a copy of the IIRUP to serve as basis in derecognizing said unserviceable PPE items from the books of account.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Accountant:
a. Regularly review the list of outstanding checks and take note of stale checks so that the appropriate adjusting entries could be made to take up the same as Accounts Payable or any other appropriate account pursuant to Section 59, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs; and
b. Prepare adjusting entry to take up the stale checks totaling ₱55,939.83 to present the correct cash balance in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Outstanding checks were regularly reviewed by the Municipal Accounting Office and adjusting entries were prepared to take up stale checks.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following remedial measures:
a. For the Municipal Health Office to provide the MAccO copies of the Invoice Receipts for Property so that the transferred drugs and medicines, medical, dental and laboratory supplies, and other supplies will be recognized in the books of the Municipality; and
b. For the MHO to prepare a report on the utilization of the commodities received from DOH-CAR to be forwarded to the MAccO, copy furnished the Audit Teams of the Municipality of Kabayan and DOH-CAR.
observations.managementAction: Copies of Invoice Receipts for property were furnished to the MAccO for appropriate recording in the books of accounts.

The MKTCAC had ceased the collection of Green and Cultural Fees. 

The MKTCAC deposited the remitted Green and Cultural Fee in the Municipality’s Trust account. 

The Municipal Treasury Office assumed the collection of the Green and Cultural Fee and were issuing the official receipts of the Municipal Government.

Subsidiary ledgers (SLs) for the Green and Cultural Fund were maintained by the MAccO under the Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management send final demand letters to the delinquent taxpayers, else, publicly advertise for sale or auction their properties to collect their tax delinquencies.
observations.managementAction: Management sent their demand letters to delinquent taxpayers thru the Provincial Government which resulted in partial settlement of their tax delinquencies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Ensure that only feasible Programs/Projects/Activities are included in the AIP. Programs and projects with problems, issues, complaints, and some other reasons should be deferred and not considered during the budget process;
b. Fast-track the procurement process; and
c. Formulate/develop specific strategies/solutions to effectively implement development projects. Consider formulating a timetable within which to start and complete the unimplemented projects.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s accomplishment in implementing project funded under the MDF had increased compared to last year. However, one hundred percent project implementation was not attained.

There were no specific strategies/solutions developed by the agency to effectively implement development projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management hire full time employees who will man the LDRRMO, in compliance with JMC No. 2014-1, to fully implement the functions of research and planning, administration and training, and operations and warning as well as to implement and monitor disaster risk reduction and management activities and projects.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRM Office and plantilla for the LDRRM Officer were created; however, there was no budget allocation for the three staff under this office and for its operation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that PPAs funded under the LDRRMF be allocated to projects, programs and activities, which contribute to the attainment of the purpose of the fund.
observations.managementAction: PPAs under the LDRRMF were in consonance with JMC 2013-01.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management enforce the immediate settlement of said disallowances and suspensions.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2013 Audit Disallowances were partially deducted from the monthly payroll of the concerned Officer.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management ensure that property numbers are assigned and property and equipment properly labelled with stickers before issuance to end-users for easy identification.
observations.managementAction: Some properties were assigned with property number and labelled after its issuance to the respective end-user.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to secure deed of donation and other supporting documents with regard to the donated assets and provide copies thereof to the Municipal Accounting Office as its basis in recording them in the books of accounts. If the cost has not been provided by the donor, have the donated asset appraised by the appraisal committee of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: Management was able to secure appropriate documents for some properties received from donors as basis for recording in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare Notice of Payment to the Accountable Officers for the lost assets/equipment and to the Municipal Accountant to set up the receivable from the AOs.
observations.managementAction: Property costing ₱24,000.00 was presented to the Municipal Treasurer. However, no Notice of Payment was issued to the concerned AOs for the remaining reported lost property aggregating ₱48,020.00.

observations.recommendation: We have reiterated that management create and establish a LDRRM Office with allocation of necessary personnel and budget through an appropriate ordinance enacted by the Sanggunian Bayan.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRM Office was created and plantilla for the LDRRM Officer were created; however, there was no budget allocation for the three staff under this office and for its operation.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management: Require the accountant to verify the details of the difference of ₱18,607.77 and effect the necessary adjusting entries; and ensure reconciliation and elimination of reciprocal accounts.
observations.managementAction: Necessary adjusting entries were made to reflect the correct balances of the reciprocal accounts of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Treasurer trace to whom the remaining 200 pieces of accountable forms were issued, since 200 pieces of accountable forms were already accounted for.
observations.managementAction: Management traced the 200 pieces accountable forms that were accounted for. The 200 pieces accountable forms are AF 51 and Community Tax Certificate issued to barangays; however, said accountable forms were yet to be surrendered to the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management give priority attention to the completion of the public market building to avoid wastage and efficient use of funds through non-utilization of the project.
observations.managementAction: Construction of the public market building was not completed in CY 2016. Management resumed construction in CY 2017.
